channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Dragon Ball Xenoverse 3 Brings Us a Bold New Future by Akira Toriyama
item.content:encoded:
Dragon Ball Xenoverse 3, formerly known as the Age 1000 project, takes place more than 200 years after the canon timeline of the Dragon Ball Super anime. It's a new world based on notes left behind by the famed author Akira Toriyama, and it stars you, a member of the Great Saiyaman Squad.

After seeing a hands-off preview of Xenoverse 3 at Summer Game Fest Play Days, I interviewed Masayuki Hirano, the game's producer. He's excited about where his team—and where players—will take Dragon Ball with this new online world.

"Xenoverse 3 takes place in Age 1000, which is a brand new world setting that Toriyama-sensei came up with, and he's done a lot of documentation of this world and the settings, the lore, and character design," he told me through a translator. "So I think there's going to be a lot to look forward to and a lot of areas to have fun with as a player."

Will Goku Fight Vegeta (Saiyan Arc) Again in Dragon Ball Xenoverse 3?

If you were wondering whether we'd relive iconic moments like Goku and Vegeta fighting on planet Earth, the answer here is a solid maybe. While this takes place in a future world, there are fan-favorite scenes that "might show up," according to the game's producer. How and why they show up is where the mystery lies.

"In the past, I know we start with Raditz and see Goku fight Vegeta, which is one of everyone's favorite scenes," Hirano-san said via his translator. "We also want to be mindful, though. Despite this being Age 1000, and knowing there are scenes people love, like if there's a first-time player, for example, how they would interact with the game.

"So yes, this does take place in a future world, but that doesn't mean to say that different beats from the anime that people know about... They might show up. How that happens, and sort of the underlying logic there, that remains to be seen."

Like previous Xenoverse games, there's some sort of gimmick that enables all sorts of fan service; for example, you were part of the time patrol, protecting the sacred timeline. Unlike its predecessors, it seems like this game could twist the formula, since you're tasked with protecting West City instead of traveling through time—though it might not step too far away from the aforementioned sacred timeline somehow.

There are still loads of familiar faces, but they show up in different ways in Xenoverse 3. For example, we saw Future Trunks from Z and Saiyan Saga Vegeta in the same space from different times, but Bulma and Gamma 1 are canonically in Age 1000 somehow.

While Hirano-san couldn't explain why these characters exist in the same space, he did chalk this up to Dragon Ball being a franchise where so many things can happen. After all, aliens and bio-creatures fight across the galaxy, die, and get resurrected by seven orange balls.

"There are certain versions or expressions of Vegeta that people love from the original anime, and yes, that exists in this world, but how I think is the big question," he said. "So what I can say is that there is an underlying logic that can explain all of that, which is also an important factor in the story of this world as well, all of which is built on top of different ideas and settings that Toriyama-sensei has left us."

If you were wondering about the manga, Hirano-san said that Xenoverse 3 is only based on the anime. However, there are plans for more Dragon Ball anime in the future, so perhaps whatever happens in those new anime projects could show up in the game in the future. After all, Xenoverse 2 had nearly a decade of post-launch support.

Squad Up & Power Up

While a lot of the story remains a mystery, we did get a good glimpse at how the gameplay looks in Xenoverse 3. You still primarily play as a create-a-character, and we saw two races to choose from: Earthlings and Saiyans. These two shouldn't be a surprise, and we were told that there would be more races. If previous games are anything to go by, Namekians are pretty likely—and if you scroll back up to the first image, you'll see why I'm placing bets on Frieza and Majin being races, too.

"Customization is an area we put a lot of strength into for this game, especially giving players a lot of agency and a wide range of things that they can customize, definitely more so than Xenoverse 1 and 2," Hirano-said said. "Part of that is the new game engine that we developed for Xenoverse 3, looking at all the player voices and feedback, and how players want to play the game, and what kind of desires they might have in this world.

"A lot of that customization is also related to how your character will fight in different situations and how you can customize your character's toolkit and loadout in order to fight with the kind of strategy you want to."

You take on missions given to you by NPCs, like how Gamma 1 did in the gameplay we saw. According to Hirano-san, Gamma 1 will be a very important character and plays a big role with the Great Saiyaman Squad. In the mission, your character joins a strike team with three others and explores the stage freely by running, jumping, flying, and of course fighting.

In our demo, we saw the player fight a bunch of Frieza soldier mobs. Each little arena had a handful, with later arenas introducing larger enemy threats. Generally though, the goal is to beat up the baddies, throw out a couple kamehamehas, and move on.

Ki plays both as your stamina and meter in Xenoverse 3. You can expend it to launch some Super Attacks, but if you run out and get hit, you get stunned for a bit. However, the same is true for enemies, and the more you hit them, the more you deplete their ki.

Of course, you can also launch an Ultimate Attack after building up enough resources. In our demo, the character used Super Kamehameha (because what else would it be, right?). While charging it, the player can aim where it'll shoot, and it ends up covering a huge portion of the screen and arena. It felt properly powerful and destructive, as it should in a Dragon Ball game.

In battle, players can also call on a character to help them out by doing a special attack. In our demo, the player called on Vegeta, who did a little assist attack for some extra damage. And by building up enough meter, the player can do a Soul Swap, where you fully control a powerful version of a Dragon Ball character for a certain amount of time. It's a nice moment of fan service that also acts as a fun-looking powerup in battle.

Saiyans, however, can transform into Super Saiyans instead of doing a Soul Swap, which makes sense for their racial traits. After some time, the player in our presentation even powered it up into Super Saiyan 3. We didn't see Super Saiyan God or Blue, but I wouldn't be surprised if they show up somehow down the line.

It was cool seeing the unique hairstyle come into play though. In his base form, the Saiyan character had a sort of bowl cut, but the Super Saiyan hair spiked up and went golden. Super Saiyan 3 was a little less impressive though, since pretty much everyone's hair in that form looks really similar. The character loses their eyebrows though, which is a nice, canonically accurate touch.

Dragon Ball Games Don't Exist Without Community

Dragon Ball has been around since the 1980s, and it's endured for decades with a somewhat constant flow of new content, even after the unfortunate death of its author Akira Toriyama in 2024. It's a widely beloved IP, which Hirano-san understands. Furthermore, he thinks that the franchise is set up in a way that makes it open to lots of interpretations in video games.

"There's been many games throughout the history of Dragon Ball, even the ones that I've been involved with—Xenoverse, FighterZ, Sparking! Zero," he said. "There are a lot of ways to interact with the world, interact with the characters, and a lot of ways to love this franchise. And that's probably something that's been built over that 40 years, and the trust with fans as well.

"Xenoverse 2 especially, we were able to support 10 years after its release with a lot of updates and content, and that's thanks to the community's support for the game."

During those 10 years with Xenoverse 2, Hirano-san and the team gathered player feedback, and overall, he found that players responded well to the franchise's direction. it gave players a new way to experience Dragon Ball, and for him, Xenoverse 3 will be no exception.

"For me, I want to find and develop different ways and environments where people can spend more time in that universe. Xenoverse specifically, because it has such a heavy online component, I almost see it as not just Bandai Namco developing a game for fans; it's almost like we're building it together and we're growing the franchise together, which is probably why it's endured for 40 years," Hirano said. "Hopefully with Xenoverse 3, we'll go into the 50th, 60th anniversary as well."

Dragon Ball Xenoverse 3 will launch on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S some time in 2027.



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/dragon-ball-xenoverse-3-brings-us-bold-new-future-by-akira-toriyama
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Star Wars: Galactic Racer May Be What F-Zero Fans Have Been Waiting For
item.content:encoded:
We've seen some spiritual successors to classic Star Wars games and series in the last several years with the Jedi series, Squadrons, and even Battlefront. Naturally, we needed a followup to the smash hit Star Wars Episode I: Racer. Podracing is back in Star Wars: Galactic Racer, and it is insanely fast.

Classic Arcade Racing With Modern Polish

Galactic Racer is an arcade racing game that is all about speed. For those looking for podracing, it is definitely in the game, but the standard racing is with landspeeders, speeder bikes, and a new type of vehicle Star Wars fans may be unfamiliar with, the skim speeder.

Each type of vehicle has its strengths and drawbacks, playing very differently. The landspeeder is your classic all-rounder that is about following the racing lines and drifting. Speeder bikes have a huge amount of acceleration and speed but they can't quite cut the close corners of other vehicles.

The skim speeder may be the most interesting of the bunch and with the highest skill ceiling. The handling is near instantaneous to your control, meaning you can turn on a dime and hug walls and corners closer than anyone. Obviously, that will come with extreme risk and the slightest twitch or miscalculation will send you careening off into an explosion.

Speed is the biggest key to all races, and Galactic Racer will feature two different types of boosts to get you there. The Afterburner is your standard boost that burns some fuel that will refill over time. Then you have a second boost, the Ramjet.

The Ramjet boosts you faster and potentially further than the Afterburner, but there can be a risk at the end of it. Using it generates a lot of heat that can lead to an explosion of you're not paying attention. You'll see a bar filling up that will eventually redline, meaning you're about to explode if you don't stop.

There are environmental effects that can help out with that boosting as well. Driving over water will naturally cool down the Ramjet burners or at least slow the speed they heat up. Drive over some lava? Well, it of course dramatically increases the heat.

The Ramjet is also one of the ways you can move a fellow racer out of the way, bumping them into a wall or hazard or just barreling ahead of them.

The speed takes some time to get used to and when you finally get the feel of a track down just right and are able to maximize your speed with just the right cornering… It feels really good.

Podracing Is Insanely Fast and Very Difficult

After I had got used to the standard racing with the above mentioned vehicle types, I gave podracing a try. It was like playing an entirely different game.

Podracing is substantially more difficult than the base racing in the game because it is so, so much faster. I was shocked at what felt like I was going twice as fast down a track, meaning you have way less time to react to what you see ahead of you. It also means that you have to be careful with even just the slightest movements as the terrain is blowing past you at an incredible rate.

I wish I was able to spend more time with podracing, but it was truly exhilarating when I was finally starting to feel more comfortable with it instead of crashing over and over. It is like threading a needle at Mach speed, and when you finally make it through a section without smashing into a wall, you feel superhuman.

Honestly, it was hard not to think of F-Zero when playing Galactic Racer, especially when podracing. It's not a one to one and I'd say F-Zero is even more arcadey than Galactic Racer, but I think this is the closest thing to that style of racing game that we've seen in a long time, especially at with this sort of budget.

The Campaign is a Runs Based Roguelike

There are a few modes to play Galactic Racer, including arcade and multiplayer, but the big mode is the campaign. I was surprised to learn the campaign is runs based with roguelike elements, meaning you shape your individual vehicle each run.

The story progresses the further you make it on a run. Runs are structured in a Slay the Spire sort of way, where there are a few paths to choose you can go down with nodes that include things like races or moments with certain characters.

The races have different criteria, like having to place top three and then there are some more arcade-like challenges. The one I saw had me using the Ramjet for as long as possible to hit a target distance before it blew up.

After each race, you'll be able to upgrade a stat of your vehicle. Those include things like upgrading the Ramjet, cornering, Afterburner, and things like that. You'll get a random selection to choose from to build your vehicle as you progress through the run.

There are also parts to select from that change how your vehicle reacts in certain environments. For example, some parts may take advantage of the more flame-filled tracks. You'll know what planet your races are on ahead of time as well, so you can try to plan ahead to take advantage.

Then there are abilities you may get as a reward as well. Abilities range from a shield that protects you for a short time, something that will heal up your vehicle, and I used one in my time that drastically reduced the cooldown of the Ramjet. 

On top of all that, there are Racer Styles that you can select and customize, each of which change the way you race. They are essentially loadouts and modifiers that give you unique traits and abilities to further make your racing unique to you.

I didn't have enough time to delve deep into all of the different ways you can customize your vehicle and racing (which of course includes visual customization), but there are a lot of categories that let you affect how you want your vehicle to work. Seemingly, there will be a lot of choices to drastically change how a vehicle will feel from run to run.

Primed For a Fun Star Wars Story

Between races and runs, there are areas on the various planets you'll be walking around as Shade, which was another surprise with Galactic Racer. It's here where you'll be able to talk to other racers, Shade's sponsor for the races, the mechanic working on your vehicles, and a whole bunch of other wonderfully Star Wars flavored characters.

The setup is that Darius Pax, creator of the Galactic League, just has a love for racing and recognizes that Shade is someone that can bring excitement to the league and be a real challenger to the champion, Kestar Bool. So, Darius decides to secretly sponsor Shade in the races.

As for Shade's part, he's an ace pilot who reluctantly turns racer as he wasn't interested at first. That is until he learns the champion of the Galactic League is Kestar Bool, the son of in the powerful Bool family who Shade has had person run-ins with in the past. Thus, a rivalry is born when Shade decides he wants to take Bool down.

Set in the time of the New Republic, these races are "unsanctioned" by the fledgling government, so racers are sponsored by wealthy groups or more often than not crime syndicates. Of course they are so we know there's chances for things like the Hutts to make an appearance.

And, of course, there are tracks all over the galaxy. There are some returning planets we know like Jakku and Ando Prime, but there are several new planets introduced by Galactic Racer. Those are Lantaana, a tropical planet with volcanos and Sentinel I, a toxic planet with acid rivers. They also introduce a new location Derven Acos that serves as the seat of the Galactic League on a planet unknown.

I can also confirm there will be at least one returning planet they've asked us not to spoil for now.

There will be returning characters like Sebulba in the story as well, and with it being Star Wars, it's hard to believe we won't have some more cameos as well. Everything I saw in my short time has me cautiously optimistic we'll be in for a fun time with Galactic Racer's interpretation of Star Wars.

Star Wars: Galactic Racer Preview | Final Thoughts

I wasn't sure what to expect with Galactic Racer, but I walked away from my time with it very impressed. At its core, it is a very fun arcade racer with a lot of customizability and modes to have a lot of fun with. On top of that, it embraces the Star Wars theming wonderfully.

The runs based design to customize your racer each time adds a nice element to make each race feel fresh, and the depth of choices in that customizability give it a long runway. I think it will be able to capture that "one more run" feeling all good roguelikes strive for.

Star Wars: Galactic Racer releases on October 6th for PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S.

Star Wars Galactic Racer was previewed at Summer Game Fest.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/star-wars-galactic-racer-may-be-what-f-zero-fans-have-been-waiting-for
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
